examples.explanation: Array: [2, 3, -2, 4], n=4. Initialize max_product = 2 (first element). Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*2=2, max_product=max(2,2)=2. j=1: current_product=2*3=6, max_product=max(6,2)=6. j=2: current_product=6*(-2)=-12, max_product=max(-12,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-12*4=-48, max_product=max(-48,6)=6. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*3=3, max_product=max(3,6)=6. j=2: current_product=3*(-2)=-6, max_product=max(-6,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-6*4=-24, max_product=max(-24,6)=6. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-2*4=-8, max_product=max(-8,6)=6. i=3: current_product=1. j=3: current_product=1*4=4, max_product=max(4,6)=6. Final max_product=6 from subarray [2,3].

Array: [-2, 0, -1], n=3. Initialize max_product = -2. i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,-2)=-2. j=1: current_product=-2*0=0, max_product=max(0,-2)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*0=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-1)=-1, max_product=max(-1,0)=0. Final max_product=0 from subarray containing 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays by iterating through each starting index and extending to all possible ending indices, computing the product for each subarray and tracking the maximum. Use this for small inputs or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Product Subarray
ladders.desc: Find the contiguous subarray with maximum product using O(n²) brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the maximum product subarray systematically examines every possible contiguous subarray in the array. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the subarray with the highest product, we can simply compute the product for every possible subarray and track the maximum value encountered. This approach works because it's exhaustive—it doesn't miss any potential solution. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each possible starting index, and the inner loop extends from that starting index to all possible ending indices. For each subarray defined by these indices, we compute the product by multiplying elements sequentially. We maintain a variable to track the maximum product seen so far, updating it whenever we find a larger product. The time complexity is O(n²) because for an array of size n, there are approximately n²/2 subarrays, and we compute each product in O(1) time per element (though we could optimize by reusing previous products). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach connects directly to the problem statement—it's the most naive but correct solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to handle empty arrays or arrays with all zeros/negatives properly, 2) Not initializing the maximum product correctly (should start with the first element or negative infinity), 3) Integer overflow with large products (though less common in Python). Real code pattern: Initialize max_product with the first element or negative infinity. Outer loop i from 0 to n-1: set current_product = 1. Inner loop j from i to n-1: multiply current_product by nums[j], update max_product if current_product > max_product. Return max_product.

examples.trace: Array: [2, 3, -2, 4], n=4. Initialize max_product = 2 (first element). Start i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*2=2, max_product=max(2,2)=2. j=1: current_product=2*3=6, max_product=max(6,2)=6. j=2: current_product=6*(-2)=-12, max_product=max(-12,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-12*4=-48, max_product=max(-48,6)=6. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*3=3, max_product=max(3,6)=6. j=2: current_product=3*(-2)=-6, max_product=max(-6,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-6*4=-24, max_product=max(-24,6)=6. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-2*4=-8, max_product=max(-8,6)=6. i=3: current_product=1. j=3: current_product=1*4=4, max_product=max(4,6)=6. Final max_product=6 from subarray [2,3].

Array: [-2, 0, -1], n=3. Initialize max_product = -2. i=0: current_product=1. j=0: current_product=1*(-2)=-2, max_product=max(-2,-2)=-2. j=1: current_product=-2*0=0, max_product=max(0,-2)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=1: current_product=1. j=1: current_product=1*0=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_product=max(0,0)=0. i=2: current_product=1. j=2: current_product=1*(-1)=-1, max_product=max(-1,0)=0. Final max_product=0 from subarray containing 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Product of All Subarrays Starting at Index i
ladders.desc: Given a starting index i, compute products of all subarrays starting at i and ending at j for j from i to n-1.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach—it handles the inner loop that computes products for all subarrays beginning at a fixed starting index i. The intuition is that by fixing the start, we can efficiently extend the subarray one element at a time, multiplying the current product by the new element. This avoids recomputing products from scratch for each ending index. The algorithm: For a given array nums of length n and starting index i, initialize current_product = 1. Then iterate j from i to n-1: multiply current_product by nums[j], and this gives the product of subarray nums[i..j]. We can track the maximum product among these subarrays starting at i. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in the worst case (when i=0), but averaged over all i it contributes to the overall O(n²). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each starting index i in the outer loop. Common mistakes: 1) Not resetting current_product to 1 for each new starting index, 2) Forgetting to handle the case when the array is empty or i is out of bounds. Real code pattern: Function compute_max_from_start(nums, i): current_product = 1, max_from_i = negative_infinity. For j from i to len(nums)-1: current_product *= nums[j], max_from_i = max(max_from_i, current_product). Return max_from_i.

examples.trace: nums=[2,3,-2,4], i=0, n=4. Initialize current_product=1, max_from_i=-inf. j=0: current_product=1*2=2, max_from_i=max(2,-inf)=2. j=1: current_product=2*3=6, max_from_i=max(6,2)=6. j=2: current_product=6*(-2)=-12, max_from_i=max(-12,6)=6. j=3: current_product=-12*4=-48, max_from_i=max(-48,6)=6. Return 6.
nums=[-2,0,-1], i=1, n=3. Initialize current_product=1, max_from_i=-inf. j=1: current_product=1*0=0, max_from_i=max(0,-inf)=0. j=2: current_product=0*(-1)=0, max_from_i=max(0,0)=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays of an array by varying start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is generating every contiguous subarray. The intuition is that to find the maximum product, we must consider all possible subarrays, as the product can vary dramatically with different segments. A contiguous subarray is defined by two indices: start i and end j, where i ≤ j. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-1 (all possible starts), and the inner loop iterates j from i to n-1 (all possible ends for that start). This generates exactly n*(n+1)/2 subarrays, covering all possibilities. Time complexity is O(n²) for generation alone, and space is O(1) if we just process indices without storing subarrays. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the i and j bounds for product computation. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop ranges, 2) Generating non-contiguous subarrays by mistake, 3) Not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: For i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): subarray = nums[i:j+1] (if needed for processing).

examples.trace: n=2. i=0: j=0 -> [a], j=1 -> [a,b]. i=1: j=1 -> [b]. Total 3 subarrays.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Product of a Given Subarray
ladders.desc: Given a subarray defined by start and end indices, compute its product by multiplying all elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the product of a contiguous subarray, which is essential for evaluating each subarray in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: the product of a list of numbers is found by multiplying them together sequentially. For a subarray nums[i..j], the product is nums[i] * nums[i+1] * ... * nums[j]. The algorithm: Initialize product = 1. Iterate k from i to j inclusive: multiply product by nums[k]. Return product. Time complexity is O(length of subarray), which in worst case is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by being applied to each generated subarray. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to handle empty subarrays (product could be defined as 1 or 0, but typically not needed), 2) Integer overflow with large products, 3) Not handling zeros correctly (product becomes zero). Real code pattern: Function subarray_product(nums, i, j): product = 1; for k in range(i, j+1): product *= nums[k]; return product.

examples.trace: Subarray: [2,3,-2]. Initialize product=1. k=0: product=1*2=2. k=1: product=2*3=6. k=2: product=6*(-2)=-12. Return -12.
Subarray: [0,5]. Initialize product=1. k=1: product=1*0=0. k=2: product=0*5=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiply a Sequence of Numbers
ladders.desc: Given a list of numbers, compute their product by iteratively multiplying.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves computing the product of a sequence of numbers, which is fundamental to the subarray product computation. The intuition is that multiplication is associative, so we can multiply numbers in any order, but typically we do it sequentially from left to right. The algorithm: Start with an accumulator initialized to 1 (the multiplicative identity). For each number in the list, multiply the accumulator by that number. After processing all numbers, the accumulator holds the total product. Time complexity is O(k) for a list of length k. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block as the core operation for computing subarray products. Common mistakes: 1) Initializing accumulator to 0 instead of 1, 2) Forgetting to handle empty lists (product could be defined as 1), 3) Overflow in languages with fixed integer sizes. Real code pattern: Function product_of_list(arr): result = 1; for num in arr: result *= num; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize result=1. Process 2: result=1*2=2. Process 3: result=2*3=6. Process 4: result=6*4=24. Return 24.
Initialize result=1. Process -1: result=1*(-1)=-1. Process 5: result=-1*5=-5. Process -2: result=-5*(-2)=10. Return 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Identify what a contiguous subarray is in an array.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept defines a contiguous subarray, which is crucial for the problem. A contiguous subarray is a sequence of elements from an array that are consecutive—that is, they appear next to each other without any gaps. For example, in array [a, b, c, d], [b, c] is a contiguous subarray, but [a, c] is not because it skips b. The intuition is that in many array problems, we care about contiguous segments because they represent continuous intervals, which often have special properties like sums or products that can be computed efficiently. To identify a contiguous subarray, you need two indices: start i and end j, with i ≤ j, and the subarray includes all elements from index i to j inclusive. The number of contiguous subarrays in an array of size n is n*(n+1)/2. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the segments over which we compute products. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing with subsequences (which can be non-contiguous), 2) Off-by-one errors in index ranges, 3) Not considering empty subarrays (though often not needed). Real code pattern: In Python, a contiguous subarray from i to j is nums[i:j+1].

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices 0:1, 1:2, 2:3. [1,3] would correspond to indices 0 and 2, but index 1 (value 2) is skipped, so it is not contiguous. Contiguous subarrays must have consecutive indices.

examples.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], list all contiguous subarrays of length 2
examples.trace: Length 2 means start i and end j=i+1. i=0: [a,b]. i=1: [b,c]. i=2: [c,d]. Total 3 subarrays.

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], how many contiguous subarrays?

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Two Variables O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain two variables tracking the maximum and minimum product ending at each index, since negative numbers can flip the sign and affect the maximum. Update these variables iteratively based on the current number and previous products to find the global maximum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Product Subarray with Two-Variable DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding maximum product of any contiguous subarray using O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The maximum product subarray problem requires finding the contiguous subarray with the largest product. The key challenge is that negative numbers can flip signs, turning a minimum product into a maximum when multiplied by another negative. The two-variable DP approach elegantly handles this by tracking both maximum and minimum products ending at each position. At each index i, we compute three possibilities: starting fresh with nums[i], extending the previous maximum product, or extending the previous minimum product (which might become maximum when multiplied by a negative). We update global_max as we iterate. The intuition is that we need to consider both extremes because in multiplication, extremes can swap roles with sign changes. The algorithm initializes max_ending_here and min_ending_here to nums[0], then iterates from index 1. For each num, we compute temp_max = max(num, max_ending_here * num, min_ending_here * num) and temp_min = min(num, max_ending_here * num, min_ending_here * num). We update max_ending_here and min_ending_here with these values, and update global_max = max(global_max, max_ending_here). Time complexity is O(n) as we make one pass through the array. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete iterative update logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle zeros properly (the algorithm naturally handles them as num will be chosen over products when zero is better), or not considering min_ending_here in the max calculation. Real code pattern: initialize variables, loop through array, compute candidates, update, track global max.

examples.trace:
Initialize: global_max = 2, max_ending = 2, min_ending = 2
Index 1 (3): candidates for max: 3, 2*3=6, 2*3=6 → max=6; candidates for min: 3, 2*3=6, 2*3=6 → min=3; update max_ending=6, min_ending=3; global_max=max(2,6)=6
Index 2 (-2): candidates for max: -2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6 → max=-2; candidates for min: -2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6 → min=-12; update max_ending=-2, min_ending=-12; global_max=max(6,-2)=6
Index 3 (4): candidates for max: 4, -2*4=-8, -12*4=-48 → max=4; candidates for min: 4, -2*4=-8, -12*4=-48 → min=-48; update max_ending=4, min_ending=-48; global_max=max(6,4)=6
Final global_max=6

Initialize: global_max = -2, max_ending = -2, min_ending = -2
Index 1 (0): candidates for max: 0, -2*0=0, -2*0=0 → max=0; candidates for min: 0, -2*0=0, -2*0=0 → min=0; update max_ending=0, min_ending=0; global_max=max(-2,0)=0
Index 2 (-1): candidates for max: -1, 0*-1=0, 0*-1=0 → max=0; candidates for min: -1, 0*-1=0, 0*-1=0 → min=-1; update max_ending=0, min_ending=-1; global_max=max(0,0)=0
Final global_max=0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Update of Max and Min Products
ladders.desc: Core function that updates maximum and minimum products ending at current index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the algorithm: at each step in the iteration, it computes the new maximum and minimum products ending at the current index. Given current number num, previous max_ending, and previous min_ending, it calculates three candidates: num itself (starting new subarray), max_ending * num (extending positive trend), and min_ending * num (extending negative trend which might flip to positive). The maximum of these becomes the new max_ending, and the minimum becomes the new min_ending. The intuition is that we must consider all three possibilities because multiplication behaves differently than addition—negative values can transform minima into maxima. The algorithm: compute temp_max = max(num, max_ending * num, min_ending * num); compute temp_min = min(num, max_ending * num, min_ending * num); update max_ending = temp_max; update min_ending = temp_min. Time complexity per step is O(1) with constant operations. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called in each iteration of the main loop. Common mistakes include updating max_ending and min_ending simultaneously without temporary variables (which causes incorrect calculations if min_ending depends on old max_ending), or omitting min_ending from the max calculation. Real code pattern: use max() and min() functions with three arguments.

examples.trace:
Candidates for max: 3, 2*3=6, 2*3=6 → max=6
Candidates for min: 3, 2*3=6, 2*3=6 → min=3
Update: max_ending=6, min_ending=3

Candidates for max: -2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6 → max=-2
Candidates for min: -2, 6*-2=-12, 3*-2=-6 → min=-12
Update: max_ending=-2, min_ending=-12

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Both Max and Min Products
ladders.desc: The insight that maintaining both maximum and minimum ending products handles negative sign flips.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to track two values at each step: the maximum product ending at that index and the minimum product ending at that index. This is necessary because in multiplication, a negative number can flip the sign: a very negative product (minimum) might become the maximum when multiplied by another negative. For example, if we have [-2, 3, -4], just tracking max would miss that -2 * 3 * -4 = 24. By keeping min_ending = -6 after second element, we can compute max_ending = max(-4, 3*-4=-12, -6*-4=24) = 24. The intuition is that extremes (max and min) can swap roles with sign changes, so we need both to consider all possibilities. The algorithm conceptually: at each index, compute potential products from previous max and min, compare with current number, and update both trackers. Time and space are O(1) per step. This connects to L1 as the foundational idea behind the update subroutine. Common mistakes include thinking only max needs tracking (like in maximum sum subarray), or not realizing min can become max. Real code pattern: maintain two variables updated iteratively.

examples.trace:
Start: max=2, min=2
Index 1 (-1): max candidates: -1, 2*-1=-2, 2*-1=-2 → max=-1; min candidates: -1, 2*-1=-2, 2*-1=-2 → min=-2
Index 2 (3): max candidates: 3, -1*3=-3, -2*3=-6 → max=3; min candidates: 3, -1*3=-3, -2*3=-6 → min=-6

Start: max=-2, min=-2
Index 1 (3): max candidates: 3, -2*3=-6, -2*3=-6 → max=3; min candidates: 3, -2*3=-6, -2*3=-6 → min=-6
Index 2 (-4): max candidates: -4, 3*-4=-12, -6*-4=24 → max=24; min candidates: -4, 3*-4=-12, -6*-4=24 → min=-12

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Product of Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Calculating products of all possible contiguous subarrays efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves understanding how to compute products of contiguous subarrays without brute force O(n²). The naive approach would consider every start and end index, multiplying all elements between them, leading to O(n²) time. The building block for our DP approach is to compute products ending at each index incrementally: product ending at i can be derived from product ending at i-1 multiplied by nums[i]. For example, for array [a,b,c], products ending at index 2 include: just c, b*c, and a*b*c. By tracking the product ending at previous index, we can compute new ones in O(1) time. The intuition is overlapping subproblems: the product of subarray ending at i often shares computation with ending at i-1. This sets up the DP structure where we maintain ending products. Time complexity reduces to O(n) if we compute iteratively. Space is O(1) for tracking current ending product. This connects to L2 by being the basis for why we track ending products (max and min). Common mistakes include not handling zeros (which reset the product) or negative signs properly. Real code pattern: initialize product = nums[0], loop and multiply by current num, reset when needed.

examples.output: Products ending at each index: index0:2, index1:3 and 6, index2:4,12,24
examples.trace:
Index 0: product ending = 2
Index 1: products ending: 3 (new), 2*3=6 (extend previous)
Index 2: products ending: 4 (new), 3*4=12 (extend from index1's 3), 6*4=24 (extend from index1's 6)

examples.output: Products ending at each index: index0:-1, index1:2 and -2, index2:-3, -6, 6
examples.trace:
Index 0: product ending = -1
Index 1: products ending: 2 (new), -1*2=-2 (extend)
Index 2: products ending: -3 (new), 2*-3=-6 (extend from 2), -2*-3=6 (extend from -2)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiplying Numbers and Comparing Values
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of multiplication and max/min comparisons used in the algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic arithmetic and comparison operations that form the building blocks of the algorithm. Multiplication of two numbers is straightforward: a * b. However, in this context, we must consider properties like sign (positive * positive = positive, negative * negative = positive, etc.) and zero (any number * 0 = 0). Comparisons involve finding the maximum or minimum among a set of numbers. For example, max(3, -1, 5) = 5 and min(3, -1, 5) = -1. The intuition is that these operations are combined in the algorithm: we multiply previous products by current number, then compare results to decide new max and min. Time complexity per operation is O(1). Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as these operations are used to compute products of subarrays and compare them. Common mistakes include integer overflow (not an issue in Python but relevant in other languages) or incorrect handling of negative multiplications. Real code pattern: use * operator for multiplication, max() and min() functions for comparisons.

examples.trace:
Multiplication: 4 * 5 = 20
Comparison: values are 4, 20, -10; max is 20

Multiplication: -3 * 2 = -6
Comparison: values are -3, -6, 6; min is -6

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Contiguous Subarrays
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of arrays and what contiguous subarrays are.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for beginners. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices. For example, [2, 3, -2, 4] is an array of 4 integers. A contiguous subarray is a sequence of elements from the array that are consecutive without gaps. For the array [a, b, c, d], contiguous subarrays include single elements like [a], [b], etc.; two-element subarrays like [a,b], [b,c], [c,d]; three-element like [a,b,c], [b,c,d]; and the whole array [a,b,c,d]. The intuition is that we are looking for one such contiguous block that maximizes the product of its elements. This sets up the problem scope: we need to consider all possible contiguous segments, not necessarily starting at the beginning or ending at the end. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 as arrays provide the numbers we multiply and compare. Common mistakes include confusing subarrays with subsequences (which don't need to be contiguous) or subsets. Real code pattern: define array as list in Python, access elements by index.

examples.trace:
Length 1: [1], [2], [3]
Length 2: [1,2] (indices 0-1), [2,3] (indices 1-2)
Length 3: [1,2,3] (indices 0-2)

Length 1: [4], [-1]
Length 2: [4,-1] (indices 0-1)

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Arrays O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two arrays to store the maximum and minimum product subarrays ending at each index, then compute the global maximum from these arrays. This approach explicitly tracks all intermediate states for clarity, though it uses more space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Product Subarray with DP Arrays
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two DP arrays to track maximum and minimum products ending at each index.
ladders.explanation: The maximum product subarray problem requires finding the contiguous subarray with the largest product. A naive approach would check all subarrays (O(n²)), but we can solve it in O(n) using dynamic programming. The key insight is that when dealing with products, negative numbers can flip signs: a negative product multiplied by another negative becomes positive. Therefore, we need to track both the maximum and minimum product ending at each position. We maintain two arrays: maxDP[i] stores the maximum product subarray ending at index i, and minDP[i] stores the minimum product subarray ending at index i. At each step, we have three choices: start a new subarray at i (nums[i]), extend the maximum product subarray ending at i-1 (maxDP[i-1] * nums[i]), or extend the minimum product subarray ending at i-1 (minDP[i-1] * nums[i]). The maximum of these three gives maxDP[i], and the minimum gives minDP[i]. We update a global answer variable with maxDP[i] at each step. The algorithm initializes maxDP[0] = minDP[0] = nums[0], then iterates from i=1 to n-1. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space complexity is O(n) for the two arrays. This approach explicitly stores all intermediate states, making it easier to understand and debug compared to an optimized O(1) space version. Common mistakes include forgetting to consider minDP[i-1] * nums[i] for maxDP[i] when nums[i] is negative, or not handling zeros correctly (which reset the product). The connection to L1 is that this level implements the full algorithm using the sub-routine of computing maxDP and minDP at each index.

examples.trace: Initialize: nums = [2,3,-2,4], n=4, maxDP = [0]*4, minDP = [0]*4, answer = nums[0] = 2. Set maxDP[0] = 2, minDP[0] = 2. i=1: nums[1]=3. Candidates for maxDP[1]: nums[1]=3, maxDP[0]*nums[1]=2*3=6, minDP[0]*nums[1]=2*3=6. maxDP[1] = max(3,6,6)=6. Candidates for minDP[1]: min(3,6,6)=3. minDP[1]=3. Update answer = max(answer, maxDP[1]) = max(2,6)=6. i=2: nums[2]=-2. Candidates for maxDP[2]: nums[2]=-2, maxDP[1]*nums[2]=6*(-2)=-12, minDP[1]*nums[2]=3*(-2)=-6. maxDP[2] = max(-2,-12,-6)=-2. Candidates for minDP[2]: min(-2,-12,-6)=-12. minDP[2]=-12. Update answer = max(6, -2)=6. i=3: nums[3]=4. Candidates for maxDP[3]: nums[3]=4, maxDP[2]*nums[3]=-2*4=-8, minDP[2]*nums[3]=-12*4=-48. maxDP[3] = max(4,-8,-48)=4. Candidates for minDP[3]: min(4,-8,-48)=-48. minDP[3]=-48. Update answer = max(6,4)=6. Final answer = 6.

Initialize: nums = [-2,0,-1], n=3, maxDP = [0]*3, minDP = [0]*3, answer = nums[0] = -2. Set maxDP[0] = -2, minDP[0] = -2. i=1: nums[1]=0. Candidates for maxDP[1]: nums[1]=0, maxDP[0]*nums[1]=-2*0=0, minDP[0]*nums[1]=-2*0=0. maxDP[1] = max(0,0,0)=0. Candidates for minDP[1]: min(0,0,0)=0. minDP[1]=0. Update answer = max(answer, maxDP[1]) = max(-2,0)=0. i=2: nums[2]=-1. Candidates for maxDP[2]: nums[2]=-1, maxDP[1]*nums[2]=0*(-1)=0, minDP[1]*nums[2]=0*(-1)=0. maxDP[2] = max(-1,0,0)=0. Candidates for minDP[2]: min(-1,0,0)=-1. minDP[2]=-1. Update answer = max(0,0)=0. Final answer = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute DP Values at Each Index
ladders.desc: Calculate maxDP[i] and minDP[i] given previous DP values and current number.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the DP approach: at each index i (i>0), we compute maxDP[i] and minDP[i] using nums[i], maxDP[i-1], and minDP[i-1]. The intuition is that the product subarray ending at i can be formed by either starting fresh at i, extending the maximum product subarray ending at i-1, or extending the minimum product subarray ending at i-1. We consider all three products: nums[i], maxDP[i-1]*nums[i], and minDP[i-1]*nums[i]. Then, maxDP[i] is the maximum of these three, and minDP[i] is the minimum. This handles sign flips because when nums[i] is negative, multiplying by minDP[i-1] (which might be negative) can yield a positive product, potentially becoming the new maximum. The algorithm: for i from 1 to n-1, compute candidates = [nums[i], maxDP[i-1]*nums[i], minDP[i-1]*nums[i]]; set maxDP[i] = max(candidates); set minDP[i] = min(candidates). Time complexity per index is O(1), and space is O(1) extra beyond the arrays. This feeds into L0 by being called in a loop to fill the DP arrays. Common mistakes include not considering all three candidates, or incorrectly ordering operations. The real code pattern uses max() and min() functions.

examples.trace: Given: maxDP[i-1]=6, minDP[i-1]=3, nums[i]=-2. Compute candidates: 1) nums[i] = -2. 2) maxDP[i-1]*nums[i] = 6*(-2) = -12. 3) minDP[i-1]*nums[i] = 3*(-2) = -6. List candidates: [-2, -12, -6]. maxDP[i] = max(-2, -12, -6) = -2. minDP[i] = min(-2, -12, -6) = -12.

Given: maxDP[i-1]=0, minDP[i-1]=0, nums[i]=-1. Compute candidates: 1) nums[i] = -1. 2) maxDP[i-1]*nums[i] = 0*(-1) = 0. 3) minDP[i-1]*nums[i] = 0*(-1) = 0. List candidates: [-1, 0, 0]. maxDP[i] = max(-1, 0, 0) = 0. minDP[i] = min(-1, 0, 0) = -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Both Max and Min Products
ladders.desc: The insight that maintaining both maximum and minimum products handles negative sign flips.
ladders.explanation: The central technique in this DP approach is maintaining two values at each step: the maximum and minimum product of subarrays ending at that index. This is necessary because in multiplication, the sign of the product can change when multiplied by a negative number. If we only tracked the maximum, we might miss cases where a negative product multiplied by another negative yields a positive maximum. For example, with nums = [2,3,-2,4], at index 2 (nums[2]=-2), the maximum product ending at index 1 is 6, but 6 * -2 = -12 is not the maximum; instead, the minimum product ending at index 1 is 3, and 3 * -2 = -6, but even that is not the maximum—the maximum is actually starting fresh at -2. However, in other cases like nums = [-2,3,-4], at index 2, the minimum product ending at index 1 is -6, and -6 * -4 = 24 becomes the maximum. Thus, by tracking both, we ensure all possibilities are considered. The logic: at each index, compute three products: current element alone, current element times previous max, and current element times previous min. Then, update max and min as the max and min of these three. This technique is applied iteratively to build the solution. Time and space are O(n) if stored in arrays. This feeds into L1 as the fundamental rule for computing DP values. Common mistakes include tracking only max or confusing when to use min. The real code pattern involves simple arithmetic and comparisons.

examples.trace: Given previous max=6, previous min=3, current=-2. Compute products: 1) current = -2. 2) previous max * current = 6 * -2 = -12. 3) previous min * current = 3 * -2 = -6. List: [-2, -12, -6]. New max = maximum of list = -2. New min = minimum of list = -12.

Given previous max=-6, previous min=-12, current=-4. Compute products: 1) current = -4. 2) previous max * current = -6 * -4 = 24. 3) previous min * current = -12 * -4 = 48. List: [-4, 24, 48]. New max = maximum of list = 48. New min = minimum of list = -4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Array Traversal with State Update
ladders.desc: Process an array sequentially while updating state variables based on current and previous values.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array from left to right (or right to left) and at each step, updating some state based on the current element and the state from the previous step. It's a common pattern in dynamic programming and greedy algorithms. Here, the state consists of two values: max_ending_here and min_ending_here, representing the maximum and minimum product subarrays ending at the current index. We initialize these with the first element, then for each subsequent element, we compute new values using the current element and the previous state. The update rule uses the previous state to inform the current state, enabling an O(n) solution. The intuition is that many array problems can be solved by breaking them into overlapping subproblems where the solution at i depends on i-1. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space is O(1) if we only keep previous state, but in our DP approach, we store all states in arrays for clarity. This feeds into L2 by providing the iterative framework to apply the max/min tracking logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop indices or not initializing state correctly. The real code pattern is a for-loop over indices 1 to n-1.

examples.trace: Start with array [1, -2, 3]. Initialize: max_ending_here = 1, min_ending_here = 1 (for index 0). i=1: current = -2. Compute candidates: current=-2, max_prev*current=1*(-2)=-2, min_prev*current=1*(-2)=-2. New max = max(-2,-2,-2) = -2. New min = min(-2,-2,-2) = -2. Update state: max_ending_here = -2, min_ending_here = -2. i=2: current = 3. Compute candidates: current=3, max_prev*current=-2*3=-6, min_prev*current=-2*3=-6. New max = max(3,-6,-6) = 3. New min = min(3,-6,-6) = -6. Update state: max_ending_here = 3, min_ending_here = -6. Final state: max=3, min=-6.

Start with array [0, 5, -1]. Initialize: max_ending_here = 0, min_ending_here = 0 (for index 0). i=1: current = 5. Compute candidates: current=5, max_prev*current=0*5=0, min_prev*current=0*5=0. New max = max(5,0,0) = 5. New min = min(5,0,0) = 0. Update state: max_ending_here = 5, min_ending_here = 0. i=2: current = -1. Compute candidates: current=-1, max_prev*current=5*(-1)=-5, min_prev*current=0*(-1)=0. New max = max(-1,-5,0) = 0. New min = min(-1,-5,0) = -5. Update state: max_ending_here = 0, min_ending_here = -5. Final state: max=0, min=-5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Product of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Multiply two numbers, handling integers and potentially negative values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is multiplication: given two numbers a and b, compute their product a * b. In the context of the maximum product subarray problem, we multiply the current array element by previous DP values to extend subarrays. The intuition is simple: if we have a product of a subarray ending at index i-1, multiplying by the element at index i gives the product of the subarray extended to include i. This operation must handle negative numbers, zeros, and possibly non-integers (though the problem typically uses integers). The product of two positives is positive, two negatives is positive, and a positive with a negative is negative. Zero times anything is zero. The algorithm is straightforward: result = a * b. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L3 as the arithmetic operation used in the state update. Common mistakes include integer overflow (if numbers are large) or confusion with sign rules. The real code pattern uses the * operator.

examples.trace: Given a=6, b=-2. Multiply: 6 * (-2) = -12. Since positive times negative is negative, result is -12.
Given a=-3, b=-4. Multiply: (-3) * (-4) = 12. Since negative times negative is positive, result is 12.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access and manipulate elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each accessible by an index (position). In programming, arrays are zero-indexed in languages like Python, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To solve the maximum product subarray problem, we need to traverse the array, access each element by its index, and possibly update values at specific indices. The intuition is that arrays allow us to represent sequences of numbers, and indexing lets us refer to individual elements efficiently. For example, if nums = [2,3,-2,4], then nums[0] = 2, nums[1] = 3, etc. We use loops to iterate over indices, typically from 0 to n-1 where n is the array length. Time complexity for accessing an element is O(1), and space is O(n) to store the array. This feeds into L4 as we use array elements in multiplication operations. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. The real code pattern involves declaring an array and using square brackets to access elements.

examples.trace: Given array = [10,20,30]. The indices are: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30. To access index 1, we use array[1], which returns the element 20.
Given array = [5,-3,0,7]. The indices are: index 0 = 5, index 1 = -3, index 2 = 0, index 3 = 7. To access index 3, we use array[3], which returns the element 7.
